critique.summary: The article 'A Journey Through Infertility' is an interactive personal narrative about the author's experience with In Vitro Fertilization (IVF), highlighting the emotional and medical journey from two perspectives. It includes acknowledgments to medical professionals, family, and friends who supported the author throughout the process.
mainPoints: The story is told from two perspectives, offering an interactive experience.
The author thanks numerous medical staff and specialists involved in their IVF treatment.
Support from family, friends, and fertility advocates played a crucial role in the author's journey.
The narrative format and illustration style were inspired by the game Monument Valley.
The article encourages readers to support the author financially if they found the story meaningful.
critique.truthfulness: The article is a personal account and does not make broad factual claims about IVF or infertility treatments that require verification. The acknowledgments of medical professionals and supporters are consistent with typical patient experiences. The mention of inspiration from the game Monument Valley for the interactive format is plausible and does not conflict with known information. Since the article is primarily a personal narrative, its truthfulness lies in the authenticity of the author's experience, which cannot be independently verified but is presented sincerely.
considerations: The article does not provide medical information or statistics about IVF success rates or risks.
It does not discuss alternative fertility treatments or broader societal issues related to infertility.
There is no mention of the financial costs or accessibility challenges of IVF.
The emotional and psychological impacts of infertility and IVF are implied but not deeply analyzed.
The interactive format may limit accessibility for some users depending on their device or abilities.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the emotional and physical challenges of infertility and IVF, with personal stories highlighting the difficulties and societal aspects. Some comments address broader issues like environmental impacts on fertility and potential policy solutions, while others critique the article's design or representation.

comments.author: looselygoosy
comments.text:
My heart skips a beat whenever I scroll past an article on infertility. It's such a lonely journey that any public mention of it gives me such a jolt.

I've experienced a surprising amount of what was outlined in the story. I'm currently battling my IVF clinic trying to obtain my own medical file without paying $40 for the privilege. I'm leaving them after four rounds of IVF; three of which I didn't get any embryos at all. My final round with them they overcharged me the cycle fee, didn't submit my scripts to the chemist resulting in multiple emails, phone calls, and eventually an in person visit, they misplaced my referral, and finally set up an ongoing monthly storage fee for the embryos that never eventuated, which took months for me to pick up on. All of this while dealing with yet another round of thousands of dollars spent and nothing to show for it.

Then you deal with attitudes from people who suggest that you are being selfish and to "just adopt" (nearly impossible in my country).

The one thing I don't share in common with the author is a support group. Mine has dried up as everyone in it has found success. There's support groups for those who have abandoned IVF; not as many for those who have been at it for years but are still willing to try. I found an infertility support forum online for my country - it had been abandoned, and I never got a response from the email address listed as contact.

I don't know if having kids is worth all of this, in addition to cost of living increases, climate change, world instability. The biological drive is a powerful one.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed personal experience with IVF challenges, including administrative and financial difficulties, which aligns with known issues in fertility treatments.

comments.author: chaostheory
comments.text:
On a related note, this is becoming a more common issue worldwide. Almost every country, even developing ones, are experiencing below replenishment birthrates. Only a few countries are spared in Central Africa, though not sure how long that will last.

https://www.imf.org/en/publications/fandd/issues/series/anal...

https://www.ssa.gov/policy/docs/ssb/v66n4/v66n4p37.html

https://www.newsweek.com/americas-population-time-bomb-18987...

IVF is also no longer something that's only for older women. Younger couples from both sexes are starting to need it.

https://tulipivf.com/news/detail/397

https://thehill.com/opinion/healthcare/4727738-people-need-i...
comments.analysis: This comment contextualizes infertility within global demographic trends and cites sources, making it a well-informed contribution.

comments.author: michieldotv
comments.text:
I get that in the West we are generally conceiving at a later stage in life than before, and that this does not improve odds of conception. However, with fertility, just as with so many things, we are individualising the problem. Here too the focus is on age. Just get kids when you're younger! What's the big deal.

Let's set aside that there are deeper sociological reasons on why young people start with kids later in life. We are not giving enough weight to all of the pollutants that we are exposed to in society, and how they affect our bodies, health and lives.

I heard Dr. Shanna Swan talk recently about the effects of hormone disrupting chemicals on (male) infertility. It's dire stuff really.

She was on the podcast promoting a recent Netflix documentary called The Plastic Detox in which they intervene in couple's lifestyles to reduce the amount of plastics and the hormone disrupting chemicals (bisphenols, phthalates, etc) they are exposed to. I highly recommend it.

There's over 70 different hormones in the human body regulating all kinds of biological functions. Fertility is only one aspect but think about the adverse effects these chemicals might be having on body weight, our sleep cycle, blood pressure, stress, libido, etc. We are prescribed drugs for related ailments at record rates. Maybe we should take a step back and treat less and prevent more.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights environmental factors affecting fertility, supported by references to expert opinions and documentaries.

comments.author: Sol-
comments.text:
Maybe countries could tackle such problems twofold:

- first, implement a nationwide social freezing program, where women in their 20s are offered to freeze their eggs at a young age for free. Such a large-scale program would probably also improve the tech and might make egg collection less intrusive.

- combined with this program, let the women who freeze their eggs opt-in into an egg donation program, where some of their eggs can be used by women with fertility problems

But as with many things fertility, seems that modern states simply do not have the capacity to seriously try anything. Who knows why that is.
comments.analysis: The comment proposes practical policy ideas to address fertility issues, showing thoughtful engagement with potential solutions.

comments.author: inglor_cz
comments.text:
I am a husband in an infertile pair which even needs surrogacy, so it is a horrendous journey. We've had 6 unsuccessful transfers so far - on average less than 1 a year (we have been trying since 2019), mostly on behalf of the surrogate mothers deciding to skedaddle randomly during the process. Although Covid didn't help either.

My wife regularly observes that this hell of a journey looks more taxing on me than her. Which is probably true.

This process is hard on the fathers-to-be as well. We do exist, we want to have kids, we are heartbroken each time as well. We also have to be careful not to hurt our wives' feelings when expressing our grief and sorrow.
comments.analysis: This personal perspective highlights the emotional toll of infertility on men, an often underrepresented viewpoint.

comments.author: incognito_robot
comments.text:
Beautiful but heart breaking website. I genuinely hope the author will be successful in starting a family.

My partner and I are currently going through a surrogacy process, and it's been a brutal multi year project that has had numerous setbacks. At this point we are just white knuckling our way forward.

I'm glad we as a society have these options available for those that need them, but man is it hard going through these processes.
comments.analysis: This comment shares a firsthand account of the difficulties of surrogacy, reinforcing the emotional and procedural challenges of assisted reproduction.

comments.author: danduma
comments.text:
The website is great!

Many of my friends have gone through IVF and still I was surprised by some weird parts of the story.

For example: "I was stabbed with 932 needles" and when you tap you find out "because I wanted to improve my odds, I went to 31 acupuncture appointments, where 687 needles pierced my underbelly, legs and head".

It is clearly established that acupuncture is placebo, but beyond whether this placebo might actually improve the odds (highly disputed), it is an elective alternative procedure with unclear benefit, not part of a standard IVF journey.

I understand the story is a very personal one, but it would be good to remember it isn't necessarily representative of most people's experience.
comments.analysis: The comment critically evaluates the use of acupuncture in IVF, correctly noting the lack of scientific consensus on its efficacy.

comments.author: hydrox24
comments.text:
This is a beautifully designed website. But I also think it's quite... problematic that the child's journey begins with the egg, as if the unfertilized egg is the child — and skips any mention of the sperm and the father.

It feels like this site is almost erasing the father from the IVF process.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid critique about representation and inclusivity in the narrative of IVF, emphasizing the role of both parents.

comments.author: tasuki
comments.text:
The cure for this is spending an afternoon volunteering in a kindergarden.

> There were the 7 a.m. doctor’s appointments before work; the dozens of days working from home in order to take all my medications; and the many times I reshuffled my travel plans.

Yes I mean just wait until you have kids. It's gonna get tougher.
comments.analysis: This comment is somewhat dismissive and less constructive, using sarcasm to downplay the difficulties described.

comments.author: prodigycorp
comments.text: pudding.cool articles are very neat, but the ergonomics of this particular article is really bad. It's been a long time, my wrists and fingers are tired, and i cant be bothered to fight to scroll down.
comments.analysis: This comment provides user experience feedback on the article's design, which is a valid but less substantive contribution to the infertility discussion.
